metadata.chiefComplaint: This tooth pain won't go away, and it keeps waking me up at night. I just want to sleep.
metadata.title: Symptomatic Irreversible Pulpitis

findings.label: Location and tooth
findings.value: Large cavity involving the left mandibular first molar

findings.label: Cold test
findings.value: Cold provokes worse pain that persists after the stimulus is removed; no exact duration documented

findings.label: Biting and percussion
findings.value: Slight tenderness with biting and percussion

findings.label: Gingiva
findings.value: No swelling, no fluctuance, and no purulence

findings.label: Face and mouth opening
findings.value: Face is normal without swelling; mouth opens normally

findings.label: Voice and breathing
findings.value: Voice is normal with no shortness of breath

examinations.title: Intraoral Examination Image
examinations.description: Left mandibular first molar and surrounding gingiva assessment.

conversation.openingGreeting: Hi. This tooth pain won't go away, and it keeps waking me up at night.

scripted.response: Hi.
This tooth pain won't go away, and it keeps waking me up at night. I can barely sleep.
It hurts in the lower-left side of my jaw, but I can't tell which tooth is causing it.
It has been worsening for four days.
Yes, it is constant now and will not go away.
It feels deep and throbbing.
It is a nine out of ten.
Yes, it starts hurting on its own even when nothing is touching it.
Yes, it frequently wakes me at night and keeps me from sleeping.
Cold drinks make it hurt more.
It doesn't stop right away. It keeps hurting for a little while after the cold is gone.
Chewing on that side is uncomfortable.
Biting or tapping the tooth is slightly uncomfortable.
No, I have not had facial or gum swelling.
No, I have not noticed pus or drainage.
No, I have not had a fever.
No, I have no trouble swallowing.
No, my voice has not changed.
No, I have no trouble breathing.
I have taken ibuprofen 400 mg as needed, but it has not relieved the pain enough.
I have no known drug allergies and no penicillin allergy.
I am otherwise healthy.
I last saw a dentist about five years ago, when I had an upper tooth extracted. I have had cavities before.
I smoke about half a pack of cigarettes per day.
I drink alcohol occasionally.
No, I do not use illicit drugs.
Yes, I want to save the tooth if possible.
I am unemployed, do not have dental insurance, and cannot afford care easily, so arranging treatment may take time.
The examination findings are available in the examination viewer.

patientChecklist.label: Opened the encounter respectfully
Asked what brought the patient in
Asked where the pain is located
Asked about the four-day course
Asked about constant deep throbbing 9/10 pain
Asked about spontaneous pain
Asked about nocturnal pain and sleep disruption
Asked whether cold worsens pain
Asked whether pain persists after cold removal
Asked about chewing, biting, or tapping discomfort
Asked about facial or gingival swelling
Asked about pus or drainage
Asked about fever
Asked about swallowing, voice, breathing, or drooling
Asked about ibuprofen use and relief
Asked about medication or penicillin allergy
Asked about relevant medical history
Asked about last dental care and prior extraction
Asked about smoking, alcohol, or drugs
Asked about affordability, insurance, or dental access
Asked whether the patient wants to save the tooth
Reviewed available examination findings

clinicalChecklist.label: Characterizes spontaneous, constant, nocturnal, deep throbbing 9/10 pain
Separates cold trigger from lingering response
Elicits medication, allergy, smoking, access, and tooth-saving context
Diagnoses symptomatic irreversible pulpitis and plans urgent definitive care

patientFacts.text: The pain has worsened over four days.
The pain is in the lower-left jaw; the patient does not know which tooth is causing it.
The pain is now constant.
The pain feels deep and throbbing.
The pain is severe, rated 9/10 (nine out of ten).
The pain occurs spontaneously without anything touching the tooth.
The pain wakes the patient at night and interrupts sleep.
Cold drinks worsen the pain.
The pain does not stop immediately after the cold is removed; it lingers for a little while afterward.
Chewing on the affected side is uncomfortable.
There is slight biting and percussion discomfort.
The patient has no facial or gingival swelling.
The patient has no pus or drainage.
The patient has no fever.
The patient has no difficulty swallowing.
The patient has no voice change.
The patient has no difficulty breathing.
The patient takes ibuprofen 400 mg as needed, without adequate relief.
The exact ibuprofen frequency is not specified; the patient only knows it was taken as needed.
Whether the patient took acetaminophen before arrival is not specified.
Antibiotic use for the current episode before arrival is not specified.
The patient has no known drug allergies and no penicillin allergy.
The patient is healthy and has no medical problems.
The patient has no contraindication to ibuprofen.
The patient has no history of opioid use, misuse, or abuse.
Surgical history is not specified.
The patient smokes approximately half-pack per day.
The patient drinks alcohol occasionally.
The patient does not use illicit drugs.
The last dental visit was approximately five years ago for an upper-tooth extraction; the patient has a history of cavities.
The currently painful tooth was not the upper tooth extracted five years ago; the painful tooth is still present.
Root-canal history is not specified.
Filling history for the painful tooth is not specified.
The patient has no fever but does not know an exact temperature.
The patient does not independently know the diagnosis.
The patient has no current dentist or appointment and says arranging care will take time.
The patient asks for an antibiotic because one helped with a prior toothache, but does not know whether it is indicated now.
Unemployment, lack of insurance, and inability to afford care create a dental access barrier; arranging treatment may take time.
The patient wants to save tooth if possible.

history.onset: Cold initially provoked the pain; it progressed over four days to spontaneous, constant pain.
history.duration: Four days of worsening lower-left dental pain.
history.pain: Constant deep throbbing 9/10 pain with spontaneous and nocturnal episodes, sleep interruption, cold worsening and qualitative lingering after cold removal, uncomfortable chewing, and slight biting/percussion discomfort.
history.medications: Ibuprofen 400 mg as needed without adequate relief.
history.allergies: No known drug allergies and no penicillin allergy.
history.medicalHistory: Otherwise healthy; no opioid-use history and no ibuprofen contraindication.
history.dentalHistory: History of cavities; unsure which exact tooth hurts; last dentist approximately five years ago for an upper-tooth extraction.
history.socialHistory: Smokes approximately half-pack per day; occasional alcohol; no illicit drugs; unemployed without insurance and unable to afford care easily; wants to save the tooth.

hpiFacts: Four days of worsening lower-left dental pain; the patient cannot identify the exact tooth.
The pain does not stop immediately after the cold is removed; it lingers for a little while afterward.
Pain is spontaneous, constant, deep, throbbing, severe, and 9/10.
Pain wakes the patient at night and interrupts sleep.
Chewing is uncomfortable with slight biting and percussion discomfort.
No fever, no facial or gingival swelling, no drainage, and no pus.
redFlags: Severe spontaneous and nocturnal pain with sleep disruption
No current abscess, spreading infection, or airway compromise
Return for new swelling, fever, drainage, dysphagia, dyspnea, voice change, or drooling
examinationFindings: Large cavity on the left mandibular first molar
Cold-provoked pain that persists qualitatively after cold removal
No swelling, fluctuance, purulence, or palpation pain
Normal face, mouth opening, and voice; no shortness of breath
expectedQuestions: Where is the pain and how long has it been present?
Is it spontaneous, constant, deep, throbbing, severe, or nocturnal?
Does cold worsen it and does it stop immediately afterward?
Does chewing, biting, or tapping hurt?
Any swelling, drainage, pus, fever, swallowing, voice, breathing, or drooling problems?
When was the last dental visit?
Do smoking, unemployment, insurance, or affordability affect access?
Does the patient want to save the tooth?
supportingInfo.diagnosis: Symptomatic irreversible pulpitis involving the left mandibular first molar.
managementExpectations: Offer appropriate analgesia and local anesthesia or dental block; the patient may decline the block.
Explain that antibiotics are not currently indicated without infection.
Arrange definitive dental care within approximately 72 hours.
Discuss root-canal treatment or extraction based on dental evaluation and the patient's desire to save the tooth.
Address unemployment, insurance, affordability, and delayed access.
Provide return precautions for new infection or airway signs.
requiredInvestigations: Review structured oral examination and cold-response findings.
treatmentExpectations: Offer local anesthesia or dental block; patient may decline.
Urgent root-canal treatment or extraction after dental evaluation.
No antibiotics for the current noninfected presentation.
referralExpectations: Definitive dental care within approximately 72 hours.
Practical access planning for an uninsured, unemployed patient.
safetyNettingExpectations: Seek reassessment for swelling, fever, drainage, dysphagia, dyspnea, voice change, drooling, or systemic illness.
learningObjectives: Recognize symptomatic irreversible pulpitis from spontaneous/nocturnal pain and lingering cold response.
Keep cold trigger and persistence separately elicitable without inventing a duration.
Avoid antibiotics and plan urgent definitive care around access barriers and the tooth-saving goal.

keyFindings: Four-day lower-left constant deep throbbing 9/10 pain
Spontaneous nocturnal pain with sleep interruption
Cold-triggered pain with qualitative lingering after cold removal
Chewing discomfort and slight biting/percussion tenderness
No fever, swelling, drainage, abscess, or airway compromise
Ibuprofen 400 mg as needed with inadequate relief
Financial and insurance access barriers with desire to save the tooth
criticalMisses: Failing to separate cold provocation from lingering response
Ignoring access barriers or the tooth-saving goal
reportData.idealSummary: A healthy 32-year-old with four days of worsening lower-left pain that is now spontaneous, constant, deep, throbbing, 9/10, and nocturnal with sleep disruption, worsened by cold and lingering qualitatively after cold removal, with a large cavity and slight biting/percussion tenderness but no infection or airway findings, consistent with symptomatic irreversible pulpitis requiring urgent definitive dental care rather than antibiotics.

facultyReview.question: Confirm that unsupported additions remain excluded unless separately approved.
